messages.author: gapi
messages.text: I have some confusion about the trace behavior in the firmware. I’ve read through the code and done some testing, and it seems that this is a one-way message. The local node informs the destination node about the path taken and the SNR along the way, but I can’t see how the reverse information is obtained. In other words, I can’t know the return path or its metrics. From my understanding, this feature seems to only help the other side to verify the path. I tested the ping function to a repeater in the meshcore-app, and it appears to use this trace mechanism, but that works because the target is a repeater, which rebroadcasts the message to come back. This makes me a bit confused about the overall purpose of this method.
messages.content_html: I have some confusion about the trace behavior in the firmware. I’ve read through the code and done some testing, and it seems that this is a one-way message. The local node informs the destination node about the path taken and the SNR along the way, but I can’t see how the reverse information is obtained. In other words, I can’t know the return path or its metrics. From my understanding, this feature seems to only help the other side to verify the path. I tested the ping function to a repeater in the meshcore-app, and it appears to use this trace mechanism, but that works because the target is a repeater, which rebroadcasts the message to come back. This makes me a bit confused about the overall purpose of this method.
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: ded (SalishMesh)
messages.text: When you get the path and SNR for a message, I.e. direct or channel. The path is not a trace packet and the SNR is from the last hop you heard.
messages.content_html: When you get the path and SNR for a message, I.e. direct or channel. The path is not a trace packet and the SNR is from the last hop you heard.

gapi

messages.author: gapi
messages.text: However, this is a one-way probing mechanism. The destination node receives the link test results, but the sender does not. From what I see in the code, the destination node simply forwards the information to the app and that’s where it ends. If the sender never receives this information, then the Trace behavior feels more like something designed for the destination node to validate the link, rather than for the sender to evaluate the path.
messages.content_html: However, this is a one-way probing mechanism. The destination node receives the link test results, but the sender does not. From what I see in the code, the destination node simply forwards the information to the app and that’s where it ends. If the sender never receives this information, then the Trace behavior feels more like something designed for the destination node to validate the link, rather than for the sender to evaluate the path.
messages.reply_html:
ded (SalishMesh)
Trace is different, sending a special packet along a set path to check the link

messages.author: nightcrawler
messages.text: It is a one-way path as you say because you specify the entire Trace path. If you want it to come back to you then you need to specify the return path part of it as well
messages.content_html: It is a one-way path as you say because you specify the entire Trace path. If you want it to come back to you then you need to specify the return path part of it as well
messages.reactions_html:
2
1

messages.author: ded (SalishMesh)
messages.text: ya out and back, but does not have to be symmetrical. Your companion has to be able to hear the first and last hop in the trace path, and each hop has to hear the one before and reach the one after.
messages.content_html: ya out and back, but does not have to be symmetrical. Your companion has to be able to hear the first and last hop in the trace path, and each hop has to hear the one before and reach the one after.

messages.author: gapi
messages.text: if that’s the case, does it mean that only repeaters can exist along the path? Since only nodes with allowPacketForward enabled will forward the packet to the next hop.
messages.content_html: if that’s the case, does it mean that only repeaters can exist along the path? Since only nodes with allowPacketForward enabled will forward the packet to the next hop.

messages.author: ded (SalishMesh)
messages.text: yes you can only trace between repeaters and room servers
messages.content_html: yes you can only trace between repeaters and room servers
